proof_stages.label: Limit-set measures and decay notions
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a convex cocompact hyperbolic surface, its limit set and Patterson-Sullivan measure, then defines the fractal uncertainty principle and generalized Fourier decay, including the basic Fourier-dimension obstruction on the possible exponent.

proof_stages.label: Generalized Fourier decay implies an FUP exponent
proof_stages.summary: A TT* and Schur-bound argument rewrites the FUP operator norm in terms of oscillatory kernels over the limit-set neighborhood, where generalized Fourier decay gives the bound beta = 1/2 - delta + epsilon_1/2 and hence a spectral-gap improvement.

proof_stages.label: Positive Fourier decay theorem for convex cocompact limit sets
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated: for every positive Hausdorff dimension delta, the Patterson-Sullivan measure of a convex cocompact limit set has generalized Fourier decay with some exponent depending only on delta.

proof_stages.label: Bourgain product exponential-sum input
proof_stages.summary: The proof will use Bourgain's theorem that products of sufficiently many independent, non-concentrated real random variables have power decay of their characteristic functions, a consequence of discretized ring/sum-product phenomena.

proof_stages.label: Schottky coding, balanced partitions, and transfer-operator invariance
proof_stages.summary: The limit set is encoded by reduced Schottky words and intervals, a scale partition Z(tau) is chosen so intervals have controlled size, and the transfer operator L_Z is introduced using Patterson-Sullivan equivariance to preserve integrals.

proof_stages.label: Expansion of the Fourier integral into concatenated word sums
proof_stages.summary: The Fourier integral is written as an integral of a high power of the partition transfer operator, producing oscillatory terms indexed by concatenations of 2k+1 words, then split into fixed odd blocks A and variable even blocks B.

proof_stages.label: Correlation reduction by squaring and Hölder
proof_stages.summary: The square of the oscillatory average is bounded by averaging squared integrals, expanding them into x-y correlations and then fixing the A-variables so that the remaining problem is to bound an absolute exponential sum over the B-variables.

proof_stages.label: Chain-rule linearization into Bourgain products
proof_stages.summary: The phase difference is linearized by the mean-value theorem, the chain rule turns derivatives along the word into a product of independent B_j-dependent factors, centering errors are controlled by the tau choice, nonlinearity gives positive box dimension, and Bourgain's theorem yields the required decay.

examples.name: Middle-third or linear Cantor measure as a no-decay contrast
examples.note: The standard Cantor measure shows that positive fractal dimension alone does not imply Fourier decay, and the linear Cantor comparison later explains why nonconstant nonlinear dilation factors are needed for the product-sum argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks the middle-third Cantor measure at frequencies like powers of three and says the Fourier transform equals one, giving no decay

examples.name: Cantor set with arithmetic-progression alphabet
examples.note: This artificial Cantor-type construction illustrates why repeated additive convolution cannot uniformly guarantee decay, motivating the use of multiplicative products and sum-product phenomena.
examples.engagement_reason: the arithmetic-progression Cantor alphabet is invoked as a possible nasty example with small additive-convolution decay but not analyzed
